data.title: Stock Market News Today, 1/20/26 – U.S. Stock Futures Plunge amid Trump's Greenland Tariff Threat
data.description:
U.S. stock futures dropped early Tuesday, starting the holiday-shortened trading week on a negative note. President Donald Trump has threatened a new round of tariffs over his demand to acquire Greenland, rattling global markets. U.S. markets were closed on Monday in honor of the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were down 1.76%, 1.51%, and 1.38%, respectively, at 3:51 a.m. EST on January 20.

Last week, all three major averages closed lower, despite the rally in semiconductor stocks on Thursday after Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) blockbuster results. The S&P 500 declined 0.4%, the DJIA fell 0.3%, and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 0.7%.

In a Saturday post on Truth Social, Trump announced a 10% U.S. tariff on eight European allies, including the UK, effective February 1. The tariffs will eventually rise to 25% on June 1 and will stay in place until the U.S. strikes a deal to acquire Greenland.

Looking forward, investors will focus on Trump's speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Wednesday, where he will discuss the Greenland issue.

The earnings season also remains in focus. Notable companies reporting this week include Netflix (NFLX), United Airlines (UAL), 3M (MMM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Intel (INTC), and Visa (V).

Notably, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up, floating near 4.28%. WTI crude oil futures were trending lower, hovering near $59.15 per barrel as of the last check. Additionally, the Gold Spot U.S. dollar price decreased to nearly $4,721 per ounce on Tuesday.

Elsewhere, European indexes opened lower today amid Trump's renewed tariff threats. European countries are reportedly discussing counter-tariffs and wider economic penalties in response.

Moreover, this morning, Trump threatened 200% tariffs on French wine and champagne after reports stated that France's President Macron refused to join his "Board of Peace" on Gaza.

Asia-Pacific Markets Fall

Asia-Pacific markets fell on January 20 due to ongoing geopolitical tensions, including those involving Iran and Greenland.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 0.29%. In China, the Shanghai Composite slipped 0.01%, while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.52%. Meanwhile, Japan's Nikkei dropped 1.11%, and the Topix declined 0.84%.

Stay ahead of macro events with our up-to-the-minute Economic Calendar — filter by impact, country, and more.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Here are Three Stocks Celebrating Anniversary of Trump 2.0
data.description:
Let's look at three stocks celebrating the anniversary of the start of Trump 2.0. For some people it might feel like only yesterday, while for others it's been more like a lifetime in the Upside Down. Today – January 20th – marks the one year anniversary of President Trump's inauguration as the 47th leader of the free world.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

His return for a second spell in office has been rather "colorful" – tariffs, military attacks on Iran, deportations, ICE, ceasefire negotiations in Ukraine and Gaza, a government shutdown, a criminal probe into the Federal Reserve and the capture of a foreign leader.

Trump's Testing Period

"It's been a testing period for consumers and businesses to put it mildly, amid extraordinary levels of unpredictable behaviour by the president which have had consequences globally," said Dan Coatsworth, head of markets at AJ Bell. "Trump's actions over the past year have, or stand to have, a profound impact on consumers, businesses, geopolitics, global trade, and more."

Some stocks have flourished during the period and other stocks have floundered. Let's look at the stocks which will be hanging out the red, white and blue in celebration.

Gold Miners

Barrick Mining (B) – Its share price has rocketed over 200% higher in the last twelve months. The main driver has been the surge in investor demand for gold as they seek a safe haven in a world of greater political and economic insecurity. Retail investors have helped gold ETF inflows like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) with central banks also scooping up more of the precious metal to bolster their reserves in difficult times. Trump's policies have also weakened the U.S. dollar and led to sticky inflation which also makes gold look more attractive.
data.author: David Craik

data.title: 3 Best Value Stocks with Over 30% Upside in 2026, According to Analysts
data.description:
Investors looking for undervalued stocks in the current volatile market may want to pay close attention to Wall Street's latest picks. These stocks stand out for their solid fundamentals, attractive valuations, and catalysts that could drive meaningful gains in the months ahead.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Before we go further, let's define value stocks. These are companies that look undervalued compared to their true worth, trading at lower price-to-earnings or price-to-book ratios. When the market eventually recognizes their real value, they can deliver strong gains.

Now, let's check the three value stocks that analysts have spotlighted. Each carries a "Strong Buy" consensus rating but also offers more than 20% upside potential from current levels.

Here Are This Week's Stocks

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) – This healthcare company is known for developing medical devices, diagnostics, and nutritional products. It has a Strong Buy analyst consensus rating and an average price target of $150.07, implying a 23.25% upside potential from the current levels. The company's P/E of 15.27x reflects a 44.2% discount to the Healthcare sector's median of 27.35.

The company will release its Q4 results on January 22. Abbott is expected to report revenues of $11.80 billion on earnings of $1.50 per share.

Carnival (CCL) – Carnival operates multiple cruise line brands across global destinations. Its average price target of $37.93 implies a 31.15% upside potential from the current levels. CCL stock has a Strong Buy consensus rating. Trading at a P/E of 14.46x, the company is valued 29.6% below the Consumer Cyclical sector's median multiple of 20.54.

Uber Technologies (UBER) – Uber provides ride-hailing and food and package delivery services. It has a Strong Buy analyst consensus rating and an average price target of $113.76, implying a 34.07% upside potential from the current levels. With a P/E ratio of 10.89x, the stock is priced at a 19.5% discount to the Technology sector's median of 13.52.

Recently, KeyBanc analyst Justin Patterson cut his price target on Uber stock to $105 from $110 but kept a Buy rating, citing strong adoption across ridesharing and delivery and a generally positive outlook.

data.title: Netflix Stock Forecast: Blockbuster Content Could Be Sidelined by the 'Ongoing Circus' of Warner Bros. Merger
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) is entering an important earnings print today as it tries to prove that its business is still healthy despite the chaos of its $82.7 billion bid for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). While the headlines are dominated by a "messy" bidding war involving rivals like Paramount Skydance (PSKY), investors are looking for proof that the streaming leader can still grow on its own. The stock has plummeted 29% since October, erasing nearly a year of gains, as the market weighs the massive price tag of the Warner deal against slowing subscriber numbers.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Netflix's Core Business Is Still a Powerhouse

Despite the merger noise, many analysts believe Netflix's core business is still a powerhouse. The company recently released the finale of Stranger Things, hosted a major Jake Paul boxing match, and streamed NFL games on Christmas. These events are expected to drive solid fourth-quarter results.

  • Earnings Growth: Wall Street expects earnings of 55 cents per share, which would be a 28% increase compared to last year.
  • Subscriber Beats: Some analysts predict Netflix added over 14 million new paid users, beating the average market expectation of 11 million.

The Biggest Threat Is the "Circus" of the Warner Deal

The biggest threat to the stock price aren't the earnings, it's the potential $82.7 billion bill for Warner Bros. Discovery. Some investors worry that Netflix is overpaying for a legacy media company, especially since Netflix has traditionally grown without making big acquisitions. Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos noted that "there is no chance that this print distracts investors from the ongoing circus" surrounding the merger. This uncertainty has turned some former fans of the stock into bears who are waiting to "short" the shares if the price pops after the report.

The Bidding War Gets Messier

Regarding the latest merger updates, Netflix is reportedly changing its strategy to beat out Paramount Skydance. Reports suggest Netflix is now considering an all-cash offer to speed up the process. While this might help them win the deal, it would also put a massive strain on the company's balance sheet. If the deal fails, some investors might actually celebrate, as it would remove the risk of a "poor transaction track record" that often haunts big media mergers.

Analysts Expect a "Banner Year" for Connected Television

Looking ahead, the company is leaning on new advertising partnerships, including a deal with Amazon (AMZN), to keep revenue moving upward. While analysts expect revenue growth to slow down slightly throughout most of 2026, they anticipate a "banner year" for connected television starting in 2027. Investors will be watching the guidance, the company's own prediction for the future, to see if the management team still believes in their organic growth levers or if they feel forced to buy Warner just to stay ahead.

Is Netflix a Good Stock to Buy?

Currently, Wall Street has a Moderate Buy consensus rating on Netflix stock based on 26 Buys, nine Holds, and two Sell recommendations. The average NFLX stock price target of $126.33 indicates 43.6% upside potential.
data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: Meta Platforms' (META) Asia Policy Chief Steps Down as Regulatory Pressures Intensify
data.description:
Meta's (META) longtime Asia Pacific policy chief Simon Milner is stepping down, marking a major leadership change at a time when the social media giant faces mounting regulatory scrutiny across the region. Milner, who is based in Singapore, said he will leave in the first half of 2026 to retire from full-time work.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Milner joined Meta (then Facebook) in 2012 and has held several senior policy roles across the UK, Ireland, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa before taking over the current role. He plans to remain for a few more months to help in identifying and transitioning a successor.

Regulatory Pressures Mount Across Asia

Milner's exit comes as Meta faces several new and upcoming regulations across the region. These include:

  • Australia: Meta is under intense scrutiny following the country's decision to restrict social media access for children under 16.
  • India, Vietnam, and Indonesia: New regulations such as Vietnam's data protection rules (effective January 1, 2026) and India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act will require deeper compliance efforts from Meta's regional policy team.
  • Taiwan and Japan: Lawmakers and financial institutions in these countries have increased pressure on Meta to address fraudulent ads and financial scams on its platforms.

Despite these challenges, the Asia‑Pacific region remains one of Meta's largest and fastest-growing markets, thanks to its young population. But it is also one of the most complex regulatory environments, with governments pushing for stricter rules on digital platforms.

UK Gambling Commission: Meta 'Turned a Blind Eye' to Illegal Gambling Ads

In a separate development, Meta is facing criticism from the UK Gambling Commission, which accused the tech giant of "turning a blind eye" to illegal gambling ads appearing across its platforms.

Regulators say Meta has not done enough to prevent unlicensed gambling operators from targeting users with misleading or unlawful ads. This issue adds to the company's growing list of global compliance problems.

Is META a Good Stock to Buy Now?

Overall, Wall Street has a Strong Buy consensus rating on Meta stock based on 37 Buys, six Holds, and one Sell recommendation. The average META stock price target of $823.63 indicates 32.79% upside potential.
data.author: Radhika Saraogi

data.title: Novartis Stock (NVS) Holds on Upbeat View of Tariff Exposure amid U.S.-EU Tension
data.description:
Swiss drugmaker Novartis (NVS) has said it expects to reduce its U.S. tariff exposure to a minimum by halfway through this year due to its agreement with the Trump administration and its expanding local production.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

CEO Vas Narasimhan stated this on Tuesday while speaking to CNBC, noting that he anticipates that the company would be able to meet all its U.S. demand from its domestic production in the country.

Novartis Wins Tariff Relief, Boosts U.S. Production

Narasimhan's comments follow Novartis' December voluntary agreement to lower the prices of its drugs in exchange for three years of tariff relief from President Donald Trump's administration. Months earlier, that is in April, the Switzerland-based pharmaceutical company also set out plans to pump $23 billion to boost its U.S. manufacturing capacity, including by establishing a $1.1 billion research and development institute in San Diego.

Novartis is one of several leading pharmaceutical giants operating in the U.S. that have entered into such arrangements with the American government. Others such as Pfizer (PFE), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Merck KGaA (DE:MRK), have also done the same.

Trump's Greenland Push Puts EU Drugmakers at Tariff Risk

Meanwhile, the chief executive's comments come at a time trade tensions between the EU and U.S. have resurfaced again.

Over the weekend, President Donald Trump threatened to impose additional tariffs on goods from eight European countries unless a deal is reached on his plan to acquire Greenland, an autonomous territory that is part of Denmark.

As of second and third quarters of 2025, medicinal and pharmaceutical products remain the European Union's largest export category to the United States by value. In addition, 43% of the active ingredients in branded drugs sold in the U.S. come from Europe, as of 2024, according to U.S. Pharmacopeia.

With an existing 15% tariff on branded medicines exports to the U.S., an additional 10% tariff — and the follow-up 25% tariff — could create extra costs for European drugmakers such as Novartis, Novo Nordisk (NVO), and Roche (RHHBY).

Is Novartis a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

On Wall Street, Novartis' shares currently have a Hold consensus rating from analysts based on one Buy, four Holds, and one Sell issued over the past three months.

Moreover, the average NVS price target of $127.75 implies more than 11% downside risk.
data.author: Solomon Oladipupo

data.title: 4 Things Investors Are Watching as Markets Reopen After the Long Weekend
data.description:
U.S. markets are reopening after a holiday, as investors return to trading following developments that emerged while markets were closed. With no trading on Monday due to the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday, markets now need to catch up on trade headlines, early earnings signals, and interest-rate expectations. Here are the key issues investors are watching as trading resumes.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

1. Trade headlines finally hit markets: Over the weekend, President Donald Trump said the U.S. would impose new tariffs linked to Greenland. He announced a 10% tariff on imports from eight European countries starting February 1, with the rate set to rise to 25% by June if no deal is reached to acquire Greenland. The comments came while U.S. markets were closed, delaying any immediate reaction. As trading resumes, investors are watching whether tariff concerns weigh on global stocks, particularly companies with large international exposure.

2. Earnings take the spotlight: With earnings season picking up, company results are expected to play a bigger role in stock moves than broader macro headlines. Several major companies are set to report, including Netflix (NFLX), Intel (INTC), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), United Airlines (UAL), 3M (MMM), and Charles Schwab (SCHW).

The focus is on company outlooks rather than past results. Investors want to know if Netflix can keep growing subscribers and whether Intel is seeing a pickup in demand. Attention will also be on how Johnson & Johnson's drug business is holding up, and on what United Airlines, 3M, and Charles Schwab say about costs, margins, and client activity.

3. Interest rates remain a key point: Interest rates remain a key factor for markets. Investors are watching the Federal Reserve closely for any hints on the timing of future rate cuts. If rates stay high for longer, growth stocks could remain under pressure. However, a softer message from the Fed could offer some support to the market.

4. AI and chip stocks remain in focus: AI-related stocks continue to draw attention, though expectations remain high. Chipmakers saw a brief boost last week after strong results from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). The company reported a 35% jump in fourth-quarter profit, beating estimates on strong demand for AI chips. Net income rose to NT$505.74 billion (about $16 billion), while revenue increased 25.5% from a year earlier to NT$1.046 trillion ($33.73 billion), also topping expectations.

Now, investors want to see if other tech companies can back up the AI story with real revenue growth, not just optimism.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Why Is Palantir Stock (PLTR) Dropping in Pre-Market Today?
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) shares opened in the red after the extended weekend, down about 3% in premarket hours. Despite no company-specific news, the decline appears tied to investors continuing to digest President Donald Trump's clash with European leaders over Greenland. Meanwhile, investors are also weighing increased scrutiny of defence contracts, after the UK minister confirmed that military data handled by Palantir remains under the Ministry of Defence's control. Year-to-date, PLTR stock is down roughly 4%.

data.title: Gold Marches Towards $5,000 as Trump Toughens Talk Over Greenland
data.description:
The gold price continued on its march towards $5,000 today as President Donald Trump stepped up his rhetoric over Greenland.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Spot gold rocketed 0.66% to a best-ever $4,726.12 in early trading.

Trump Threats Rattle Investors

Donald Trump's threats to take control of Greenland, currently run by Denmark, either through a financial transaction or potentially by military force, continued to rattle investors.

Speaking to reporters ahead of his trip to the Davos summit in Switzerland this week, Trump, who has threatened European allies with additional tariffs of up to 25%, said: "I don't think they're going to push back too much. Look, we have to have it. As I expressed to everyone, very plainly, Greenland is imperative for National and World Security. There can be no going back — On that, everyone agrees!"

European politicians have been left shocked and bemused by the President's stance, which potentially threatens the future of the NATO alliance and decades, if not centuries in the case of the U.K., of close cooperation.

European leaders are playing tough, threatening retaliatory tariff actions, with France requesting the activation of EU's anti-coercion tool, which could see U.S. goods banned from the single market.

Sow Trade Chaos

Wealth Club chief investment strategist Susannah Streeter said: "President Trump's willingness to break old alliances and sow trade chaos, to further what he believes to be in the US interests, are causing anxiety among investors. With both sides so entrenched, negotiation for now looks highly difficult, and it's likely to keep investor nerves on edge."

Gold is traditionally seen as a safe haven for investors in times of political and economic uncertainty. 2025 saw plenty of that with Trump's initial tariff wave and wars in the Middle East and Ukraine.
data.author: David Craik
